input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
**Physical Twin System: BIM-Based Infrastructure Assets**
  - **Type of System**: Infrastructure assets in the **Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO)** domain, particularly **buildings and construction projects**.
  - **Main Components**:
    - **BIM (Building Information Modeling) models**: Semantically rich, object-based 3D models incorporating geometric, spatial, functional, and performance characteristics of building components.
    - **Physical assets**: Real-world infrastructure elements (e.g., structures, systems, equipment) within buildings.
  - **Operational Domain**: Entire **building life cycle**, from design and construction to operation, maintenance, and decommissioning.
  - **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
    - **Semantic richness**: Integration of multi-faceted data (geometry, spatial relationships, functional attributes) for interoperability.
    - **Collaborative coordination**: Enables real-time clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation generation among stakeholders (architects, engineers, contractors).
    - **Policy-driven adoption**: Supported by regulatory mandates accelerating BIM integration in AECO.
    - **Lifecycle-centric**: Aligns with industry standards (e.g., Model-Based Systems Engineering) for scalable asset mapping.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
**Digital Twin Services**:
  - **Monitoring**:
    - Real-time synchronization between physical assets and digital twins via **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** (enabling live data integration).
    - Continuous tracking of asset performance, operational status, and environmental conditions.
  - **Optimization**:
    - Model-driven automation framework supports **scalable, service-oriented DT development** (e.g., predictive maintenance, resource allocation optimization).
    - Extensible architecture (via **modular architecture with defined extension points**) for custom optimization workflows.
  - **Prediction**:
    - Enabled by real-time data integration and BIM semantics, facilitating **performance simulations** and **clash detection** during design phases.
  - **Control**:
    - Operational control DTs allow **automated deployment processes** (reducing manual intervention) and **reproducible setups** (via **C2**).
    - Supports **semantic integration** between design-time models and runtime operations.
  - **Visualization**:
    - Collaborative platform for **3D model visualization**, spatial analysis, and documentation generation (leveraging BIM).
  - **Target Users**:
    - Architects, engineers, construction professionals, facility managers, and operational teams.
    - Domain-wide stakeholders in AECO projects.
  - **Service Capabilities**:
    - **Interoperability**: Seamless integration with BIM tools and industry standards.
    - **Real-time connectivity**: Low-latency synchronization via **Eclipse Hono/Ditto**.
    - **Extensibility**: Service-oriented architecture for plugging in domain-specific logic (e.g., AECO-specific controls).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
**Tooling and Enablers**:
  - **Software Platforms**:
    - **Eclipse Hono**: Open-source framework for real-time device connectivity and data synchronization.
    - **Ditto**: Lightweight IoT messaging protocol for live DT synchronization.
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **Model-Driven Automation Framework**: Enables **semantic integration**, **real-time connectivity**, and **extensibility** via DSR (Design Science Research) methodologies.
    - **BIM Tools**: Industry-standard platforms (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) for creating semantically rich models.
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **BIM-based simulation engines** (e.g., EnergyPlus, Dynamo) for performance analysis and predictive modeling.
  - **Databases**:
    - **Semantic databases** (e.g., GraphDB, Neo4j) to store and query relationships between BIM objects and physical asset data.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Modular Architecture**: Defined extension points for scalable, service-oriented DT development (e.g., **C4**).
    - **Design Science Research (DSR)**: Methodology for artifact development (requirements elicitation → prototyping → experimentation).
    - **Automated Deployment Tools**: Reduce manual intervention in DT setup (e.g., **C2**).
  - **Key Roles**:
    - **Eclipse Hono/Ditto**: Enable **real-time data integration** and live synchronization.
    - **BIM Models**: Provide the **semantic foundation** for DT semantics.
    - **Model-Driven Framework**: Facilitates **automation**, **extensibility**, and **reproducibility**.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities** for continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **sub-second latency** for critical operational monitoring and control. The framework leverages **runtime configuration management** to dynamically adjust synchronization frequencies and temporal granularity based on service-specific requirements, enabling adaptive temporal alignment between physical and digital components. While exact frequencies are not specified, the emphasis on **bidirectional data exchange** implies near-real-time or ultra-low-latency synchronization, critical for AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) applications where operational decisions must be made in milliseconds for system responsiveness. The modular architecture supports custom service integration, allowing tailored time-scale adjustments for different operational phases (e.g., predictive maintenance vs. real-time occupancy monitoring).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system supports a **centralized architecture** with a **single, unified DT Framework** acting as a central hub, coordinating multiple digital twin instances derived from enriched BIM models. **Facility Managers** interact with a single deployed DT for real-time monitoring, while **DT Engineers** extend capabilities through service integration, implying a **hierarchical, role-based multiplicity** where responsibilities are scoped per user role (e.g., Building Control Engineers for device modeling, Facility Managers for operational oversight). The system does not explicitly mention decentralized architectures or multiple independent DT instances, but the modular extension points suggest potential for **scalable multiplicity** via service composition. Coordination mechanisms rely on the DT Framework’s bidirectional data exchange, ensuring synchronized operational states across all components. **Not in Document** for explicit mention of coordination protocols or granularity of multiplicity (e.g., per-zone vs. per-building).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin constellation is structured around a **coordinated role-based system** where the **DT Framework** serves as the core orchestrator, facilitating seamless integration of roles: **Building Control Engineers** (BIM-based device modeling), **Facility Managers** (real-time monitoring/control), and **DT Engineers** (service extension). The architecture employs **modular architecture with standardized extension points**, enabling dynamic evolution of the DT life cycle without full redeployment. Component integration follows a **BIM-to-Operational DT flow**, where enriched BIM models are enriched with real-time operational data, ensuring alignment between static (BIM) and dynamic (operational) representations. Resource management is implicit in the **runtime configuration management**, allowing adaptive allocation of computational and data resources. System-wide coordination is achieved through the **bidirectional data exchange** mechanism, ensuring holistic building life-cycle management. The framework’s emphasis on **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** suggests a constellation where disparate data sources (e.g., IoT sensors, legacy systems) are harmonized via standardized protocols.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems via standardized extension points** and **interoperability protocols**, enabling bidirectional data exchange with heterogeneous AECO systems. Dependencies are managed through the **modular architecture**, allowing seamless incorporation of custom services and analytics without disrupting core functionality. Integration patterns include:
  1. **BIM-to-DT synchronization**: Enriched BIM models (static) are dynamically updated with operational data (real-time).
  2. **Enterprise system interoperability**: The framework supports integration with enterprise-level systems (e.g., facility management platforms) via **low-overhead updates** and **scalable architecture**, ensuring compatibility with legacy and modern tools.
  3. **Cross-DT coordination**: While not explicitly detailed, the centralized DT Framework implies potential for **orchestration between multiple Digital Twins** (e.g., per-building or per-zone twins) via shared data streams and role-based access controls.
  The system prioritizes **maintainability** and **accessibility** for non-technical users (e.g., Facility Managers), leveraging **interactive visualization** tools (e.g., immersive interfaces) for seamless integration with end-user workflows. **Not in Document** for specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, REST APIs) or detailed dependencies on external systems beyond AECO domain tools.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to construct and manage computational models and data components. The framework integrates **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** as a foundational tool for device modeling, enabling **Building Control Engineers** to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within BIM environments. This approach ensures a unified semantic foundation by extracting structured representations from enriched BIM models, facilitating seamless transformation into deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Key model types include:
- **Geometric models**: Represented through BIM’s spatial and structural data, enabling precise alignment between physical and digital building components.
- **Behavioral models**: Implemented via control logic defined by Building Control Engineers, capturing dynamic interactions (e.g., sensor-actuator feedback loops).
- **Analytical models**: Derived from MDE-driven transformations to automate the generation of deployable DT artifacts, ensuring consistency across the life cycle.

Data management is governed by **real-time synchronization** mechanisms to maintain bidirectional alignment between physical infrastructure and its digital twin. Data sources include:
- **BIM-based models** (static and dynamic components).
- **Custom services/plugins** (DT Engineers’ extensions for analytics and control logic).

Model relationships are enforced through **standardized transformation pipelines**, ensuring interoperability between BIM, MDE-generated artifacts, and runtime DT components. Data management approaches emphasize **modularity** and **scalability**, supporting extension via standardized extension points for custom services (e.g., TwinLight/BOREALIS research projects).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The framework addresses fidelity and validity through a **multi-layered validation framework** rooted in MDE and BIM integration. Key considerations include:

1. **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
   - **BIM-to-DT Transformation**: Ensures alignment between static BIM representations and dynamic DT components by leveraging structured MDE principles, mitigating BIM’s limitations in representing dynamic building control systems (BCS).
   - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Critical for maintaining fidelity during operational phases, with emphasis on minimizing latency in data exchange between physical and digital twins.

2. **Validation and Verification Methods**:
   - **Automated Artifact Generation**: MDE-driven pipelines validate DT artifacts by cross-checking against BIM models, ensuring correctness in sensor/actuator definitions and control logic.
   - **Runtime Monitoring**: Facilitated by **Facility Managers’ interactions**, where system states are continuously validated against predefined operational thresholds.

3. **Uncertainty Quantification**:
   - Implicitly addressed through **modular extension points** for custom services (DT Engineers), allowing adaptive incorporation of uncertainty-aware analytics (e.g., probabilistic models for sensor/actuator behavior).
   - **Theoretical Considerations**: Grounded in research projects (TwinLight/BOREALIS) to refine validation methodologies for dynamic DTs.

4. **Error Detection and Correction**:
   - **Real-Time Feedback Loops**: Enabled by interactive visualization tools, where discrepancies between physical and digital states trigger automated or manual corrections.
   - **Quality Assurance Processes**: Embedded in the life-cycle framework, ensuring compliance with operational requirements (e.g., safety constraints) via standardized extension points.

The framework’s modular architecture supports **iterative validation**, allowing continuous refinement of model fidelity as operational requirements evolve.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** mechanism leverages **real-time data exchange** to synchronize building operational data with its digital twin (DT) counterpart. Data flows originate from **BIM (Building Information Modeling) tools** used by **Building Control Engineers**, where device-level models are deployed into the DT framework. Key data types transmitted include operational metrics derived from physical building systems, such as sensor readings (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy, energy consumption), control signals, and status updates. The framework supports **bidirectional data synchronization**, ensuring the digital twin remains dynamically aligned with real-world conditions.

  **Communication protocols** are implied to facilitate seamless data transmission, though not explicitly named in the documents. The system emphasizes **low-latency synchronization** to enable **immersive visualization** for **Facility Managers** and **interactive monitoring/control**. Data quality assurance is implied through **runtime synchronization mechanisms**, ensuring consistency between physical and virtual states without requiring manual intervention. Preprocessing steps likely include normalization, filtering, and validation to mitigate noise or anomalies before integration into the DT’s operational layer. The modular architecture supports **custom data integration services**, allowing extension via standardized extension points to accommodate diverse data sources (e.g., IoT sensors, legacy systems).

  Event triggers are not explicitly detailed, but the framework’s **modular design** suggests support for **asynchronous and event-driven updates** to reflect physical changes in real-time. The documents do not specify transmission frequencies or protocols, but the emphasis on **continuous synchronization** implies high-frequency updates (e.g., milliseconds to seconds) to maintain temporal accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s technical connection** relies on a **centralized, modular architecture** hosted within the DT framework, facilitating **bidirectional data exchange** between physical and virtual systems. While the documents do not specify explicit protocols, the system’s emphasis on **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** suggests support for **standardized communication interfaces** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, or REST APIs) to ensure compatibility with BIM tools and IoT devices.

  The **network topology** is conceptualized as a **hub-and-spoke model**, where the DT framework acts as the central hub, distributing commands and data flows to distributed components (e.g., building control systems, sensors). **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are implied to be critical for real-time monitoring, though exact specifications (e.g., Mbps, latency thresholds) are not provided. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are likely implemented via **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic adjustments to maintain system stability without full redeployment. Security protocols are implied to address **interoperability challenges**, though no specific measures (e.g., TLS encryption, authentication standards) are detailed.

  The framework’s **modular extension points** suggest support for **dynamic network adaptation**, allowing integration of custom services and analytics without altering core infrastructure. The absence of explicit details on **network topology** (e.g., cloud-edge hybrid, on-premise) or **specific protocols** (e.g., TCP/IP, custom protocols) leaves these aspects as implicit design choices aligned with the AECO domain’s operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin hosting and deployment** infrastructure is designed as a **scalable, runtime-configurable framework** that bridges the gap between **static BIM models** and dynamic operational requirements. Deployment occurs via a **modular, extension-driven approach**, where **Building Control Engineers** integrate device models into the DT framework using BIM tools (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD). The system supports **on-premise, cloud, or edge deployment models**, with computational and storage requirements tailored to the AECO domain’s needs (e.g., handling large-scale building data).

  **Scalability mechanisms** are enabled through **low-overhead updates** and **runtime configuration management**, allowing incremental adjustments without full redeployment. The framework’s **centralized hub architecture** ensures **high availability and reliability**, with implied redundancy for critical components. The documents do not specify exact hardware/software requirements (e.g., CPU, RAM, storage capacity), but the emphasis on **maintainability** suggests support for **containerized or microservices-based deployment** to streamline updates. The **immersive visualization** and **interactive control** interfaces imply a **user-accessible layer** that accommodates varying technical expertise, likely via web-based or VR/AR interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
**Lifecycle Phases and Representation in AECO Digital Twins:**
  The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) in the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO) domain spans **development, construction, operation, and decommissioning**, with distinct representation types and governance approaches at each stage.

  - **Development Phase:**
    - **Representation:** Early DTs are modeled using **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** standards (e.g., IFC, COBie) to align physical and digital asset data. The focus is on **modular construction workflows**, where DTs enable real-time synchronization between digital blueprints and physical assets via sensor networks and AI-driven validation.
    - **Transition Management:** Manual configuration and integration of analytics (e.g., via frameworks like **Eclipse Ditto** or **BaSyx**) are required to bridge proprietary BIM ecosystems (e.g., Bentley’s **iTwin.js**) with open-source or custom components. This phase emphasizes **stakeholder alignment** to ensure consistency across engineering workflows.

  - **Construction Phase:**
    - **Representation:** DTs adopt **real-time sensor data** (e.g., IoT-enabled monitoring) to track progress, detect deviations from plans, and automate quality checks. Bidirectional communication protocols (e.g., **BIM 360, OPC UA**) ensure synchronized updates between physical and digital states.
    - **Lifecycle Governance:** Organizational barriers (e.g., lack of unified workflows) persist, necessitating **cross-disciplinary collaboration** and **cost-optimized deployment strategies** to mitigate fragmentation.

  - **Operation Phase:**
    - **Representation:** DTs evolve into **asset performance monitors**, leveraging AI for predictive maintenance, operational efficiency modeling, and cost forecasting. Integration with **BIM and IoT** ensures continuous data flow for lifecycle management.
    - **Transition:** Scalable DT implementations require **standardized data schemas** and **robust integration frameworks** to avoid case-specific silos (e.g., fragmented deployments cited in [7], [22]).

  - **Decommissioning Phase:**
    - **Representation:** DTs support **end-of-life asset analysis**, including data archiving and disposal planning. Governance focuses on **compliance frameworks** (e.g., ISO standards for digital preservation) to ensure legacy data integrity.
  **Key Limitation:** Absence of **formalized lifecycle governance models** or automated transition protocols between phases, compounded by high implementation costs and stakeholder resistance ([23], [28]).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
**Engineering and Development Methodologies for AECO Digital Twins:**
  The twinning process integrates **BIM, AI, and sensor networks** to enable real-time synchronization, but remains constrained by manual workflows and proprietary tool dependencies.

  - **Methodologies:**
    - **Modeling:** DTs are constructed via **BIM standards** (e.g., IFC, Industry Foundation Classes) for modular construction and infrastructure monitoring. Frameworks like **Eclipse Ditto** and **BaSyx** provide infrastructure-level components but require **manual configuration** for analytics orchestration.
    - **Quality Assurance:** Validation relies on **BIM 360/OPC UA protocols** for bidirectional data exchange, though **automation gaps** persist (e.g., Bentley’s **iTwin.js** demands extensive manual effort due to lack of BIM-native integration).
    - **Testing/Validation:** Real-time synchronization is validated through **sensor network integration** (IoT) and AI-driven anomaly detection, but **out-of-the-box usability** is limited (e.g., frameworks lack non-developer-friendly interfaces).
    - **Version Control:** No explicit mention of versioning systems (e.g., Git for DT models), though **fragmented deployments** suggest ad-hoc updates without standardized workflows ([25]).
    - **Evolution Management:** DTs evolve via **custom integration** (e.g., hybrid proprietary/open-source stacks) but lack **unified governance** or **scalable lifecycle frameworks** to support modular updates ([13], [29]).

  **Technologies Mentioned:**
  - **Eclipse Ditto**, **BaSyx** (infrastructure-level components).
  - **Bentley iTwin.js** (proprietary BIM ecosystem).
  - **BIM 360/OPC UA** (bidirectional protocols).
  - **IoT/Sensor Networks** (real-time monitoring).
  - **AI/Analytics** (predictive modeling, cost optimization).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
**Decision Support Capabilities in AECO Digital Twins:**
  DTs generate **operational insights** via BIM, AI, and sensor data but lack standardized confidence measures or automated decision workflows.

  - **Insights Generated:**
    - **Real-Time Monitoring:** Sensor networks (IoT) provide **modular construction progress tracking** and **deviation detection** (e.g., OPC UA/OPC DA protocols).
    - **Life Cycle Analytics:** AI-driven models forecast **costs, efficiency, and maintenance** (e.g., predictive analytics for infrastructure assets).
    - **Operational Efficiency:** DTs enable **cross-disciplinary collaboration** (e.g., BIM 360 integration) but **decision confidence** is implicit (no explicit metrics like Bayesian uncertainty scores).
  - **Analytics/Processing:**
    - **BIM + AI Hybrids:** Frameworks like **iTwin.js** process BIM data with embedded analytics, but **orchestration is manual** (e.g., BaSyx requires custom scripting).
    - **User Interaction:** Limited to **developer-friendly APIs** (e.g., Eclipse Ditto) or **proprietary dashboards** (Bentley), with **low usability** for non-technical stakeholders.
  - **Decision Processes:**
    - **Stakeholder Alignment:** Decisions depend on **consensus-driven workflows** (e.g., modular construction teams) but **automation is absent** ([23]).
    - **Recommendation Confidence:** No mention of **confidence thresholds** or **risk assessment frameworks** (e.g., ISO 31000 for decision support).
  **Key Limitation:** Insights are **case-specific** and **lack standardized confidence metrics**, hindering scalability ([7], [22]).
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
**Standards Compliance and Interoperability in AECO Digital Twins:**
  AECO DTs operate within fragmented ecosystems, relying on **BIM standards** and **proprietary frameworks** but lacking unified compliance frameworks.

  - **Industry Standards:**
    - **BIM Standards:** **IFC (Industry Foundation Classes)**, **COBie** (Construction Operations Building Information Exchange) for data exchange.
    - **IoT/Sensor Protocols:** **OPC UA (OPC Unified Architecture)** for bidirectional communication between physical and digital assets.
    - **AECO-Specific:** No explicit mention of **AECO-specific DT standards** (e.g., beyond BIM), though **Bentley iTwin.js** integrates with proprietary BIM ecosystems.
  - **Specifications:**
    - **Open-Source Frameworks:** **Eclipse Ditto** and **BaSyx** comply with **OASIS standards** (e.g., OPC UA) but require **manual configuration** for BIM interoperability.
    - **Certification:** No **DT-specific certification bodies** are cited; compliance relies on **BIM 360/OPC UA compliance** for real-time synchronization.
  - **Compliance Frameworks:**
    - **Interoperability:** **Fragmented deployments** lack **standardized data schemas**, leading to **silos** ([7], [25]).
    - **Governance:** **Unified engineering workflows** are absent, exacerbating **high implementation costs** ([28]).
  - **Adopted Standards:**
    - **BIM 360**, **OPC UA**, **IFC**, **COBie** (primary).
    - **Open-Source Protocols:** **OASIS** (for BaSyx/Eclipse Ditto).
  **Key Limitation:** **No unified DT governance framework** or **AECO-specific certification** exists; adoption depends on **case-specific adaptations** ([2], [11]).
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The **Digital Twin (DT) systems** in the **Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO)** domain, as documented, prioritize **runtime adaptability, semantic coherence, and tool interoperability** to mitigate risks and ensure safety-critical operations. Key technical considerations include:

1. **Semantic Drift Mitigation**: The alignment between digital and physical models is explicitly addressed through **synchronization techniques** to prevent divergence over time, as highlighted by Abbasi et al. [49]. This involves dynamic model updates and rule-based management to maintain integrity, particularly in complex AECO environments where physical and digital representations must remain synchronized for accurate performance analysis and decision-making.

2. **Ontological Frameworks for Service Integration**: Oakes et al. [51] emphasize the use of **ontological frameworks** to support service-oriented DT architectures. These frameworks enable structured communication across disciplines and life cycle phases, ensuring that safety-critical systems adhere to formalized governance policies encoded into domain-specific modeling languages (DSMLs) and metamodels.

3. **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) for Governance**: MDE is leveraged to abstract and formalize organizational policies and architectural decisions into **metamodels and DSMLs**, promoting compliance, reuse, and quality assurance. This approach reduces cognitive overload in large-scale AECO systems by standardizing workflows and enforcing consistency across stakeholders (e.g., BIM, IoT, and simulation platforms).

4. **Cybersecurity and Access Control**: While not explicitly detailed in the documents, the context implies reliance on **proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js [32]) or custom-built solutions that inherently incorporate **access control mechanisms** and **authentication systems** to secure data flows between DT components and external platforms (e.g., BIM, IoT). The lack of explicit mention of **data protection regulations** or **consent management** suggests these are either assumed to be handled by commercial tools or are not a focal point in the surveyed AECO DT implementations.

5. **Real-Time Data Validation and Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: The emphasis on **automated data acquisition, normalization, and real-time visualization** (e.g., in Gerber et al.’s [33] Living Lab DT) implies integration with **IoT platforms** to validate data integrity in real-time. Fail-safe mechanisms are implied for critical operations (e.g., indoor environmental optimization in Opoku et al.’s [34] library DT), though specific protocols (e.g., TLS, encryption) or risk assessment methodologies (e.g., ISO/IEC 27001) are not detailed.

**Technologies/Methods Mentioned**:
- **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** for model-driven DT integration.
- **IoT platforms** for real-time data acquisition.
- **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** via DSMLs/metamodels.
- **Semantic drift synchronization** (rule-based management).
- **Ontological frameworks** for service interoperability.
- **Proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) with assumed security controls.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],
block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime
block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
